basic_info.name: Cosmetic Emulsion Stability
basic_info.name_ja: 化粧品エマルション安定性
basic_info.description: The HSP balance between oil phase, emulsifier, and aqueous phase predicts emulsion type (O/W or W/O), thermodynamic stability, and shelf life of cosmetic formulations. By matching the emulsifier HSP to the oil phase or water phase, formulators can control which phase the emulsifier preferentially partitions into, determining emulsion type per Bancroft's rule. The HSP distance between oil and emulsifier governs interfacial tension reduction and long-term resistance to coalescence, creaming, and Ostwald ripening.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Emulsion stability is governed by the emulsifier's affinity for each phase. When the emulsifier HSP is closer to the oil phase HSP, it preferentially resides at the interface with its hydrophobic portion in oil, favoring W/O emulsions. When closer to the aqueous phase, O/W emulsions form. The Ra between emulsifier and oil phase correlates with the effectiveness of interfacial film formation: lower Ra means better wetting and packing of emulsifier at the oil-water interface. For multi-oil systems, each oil component's HSP distance from the emulsifier affects the overall interfacial stability. The concept extends the HLB (hydrophilic-lipophilic balance) framework by providing a three-dimensional matching capability that accounts for dispersion, polar, and hydrogen bonding contributions independently.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra_oil-emulsifier = sqrt(4*(dD_oil-dD_emul)^2 + (dP_oil-dP_emul)^2 + (dH_oil-dH_emul)^2); RED = Ra/R0; Emulsion type predicted by comparing Ra(oil-emulsifier) vs Ra(water-emulsifier)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: RED < 1.0 between emulsifier and dispersed phase for stable emulsion; Ra(oil-emulsifier) < 5 MPa^0.5 for excellent stability

required_inputs: Oil phase HSP (dD, dP, dH) - individual oils and blend average
Water phase HSP (dD=15.5, dP=16.0, dH=42.3 MPa^0.5)
data_sources: Cosmetic ingredient supplier literature (BASF Care Chemicals, Evonik, Croda)
Published HSP for common cosmetic oils (Brouwer 2011, Abbott & Hansen)
Group contribution estimation for novel emollients
Experimental determination via emulsion stability testing with solvent probes
complementary_parameters: Interfacial tension (pendant drop or spinning drop)

limitations: HSP does not capture the kinetic aspects of emulsification (shear rate, mixing energy, droplet breakup dynamics)
Emulsifier partitioning depends on concentration-dependent self-assembly (micelles, liquid crystals) not described by HSP
Complex emulsifier mixtures (co-emulsifiers, polymeric thickeners) create synergies not predictable from individual HSP
Temperature-induced phase inversion (PIT method) involves dynamic HSP changes that static values cannot fully model
Pickering emulsions (particle-stabilized) require surface energy and contact angle data beyond HSP
Protein and polysaccharide emulsifiers have conformational HSP that depends on pH and ionic strength
known_failure_modes: Silicone oils with very low polarity where conventional HSP matching underperforms specialized silicone surfactants
Emulsions stabilized primarily by rheological means (high viscosity continuous phase) rather than interfacial chemistry
Phase inversion emulsions where the desired type is thermodynamically unfavored but kinetically stabilized
Multiple emulsions (W/O/W, O/W/O) where two different interfaces must be simultaneously optimized
validation_method: Visual and microscopic assessment of emulsion type (dye test, conductivity)
Droplet size distribution measurement over time (Mastersizer, DLS)
Rheological measurement (viscosity and viscoelasticity changes over time)
Shelf life testing at 25C, 40C, and freeze-thaw conditions

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Brouwer, W.M.; van Haeren, P.J.C.
key_references.title: Application of Hansen solubility parameters in the development of cosmetic emulsions

key_references.author: Vaughan, C.D.
key_references.title: Using solubility parameters in cosmetics formulation
key_references.journal: Journal of the Society of Cosmetic Chemists

literature.regulatory_relevance: Cosmetic emulsion stability is critical for regulatory compliance under EU Cosmetics Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009, which requires demonstrated product stability. HSP-guided formulation helps ensure shelf life compliance. FDA OTC monograph products also require stability data. HSP-based screening supports reformulation for REACH-compliant ingredient substitution.
